summary: This article presents a structured workflow called "Research, Review, Rebuild" for migrating legacy codebases using Generative AI augmented with Model Context Protocol (MCP). The author demonstrates this approach by modernizing Bahmni, an open-source hospital management system, migrating its AngularJS frontend to React/TypeScript while also transitioning from OpenMRS REST APIs to FHIR standards. The workflow emphasizes thorough analysis of existing code through MCP servers, expert validation of AI-generated plans, and disciplined code generation with human oversight. The complete migration of a display control took approximately 10 minutes of code generation at $2 cost, compared to 2-3 days of manual development, achieving 95% test coverage.
key_points: MCP (Model Context Protocol) enables LLMs to access legacy codebases and documentation as context, transforming GenAI from a greenfield-only tool into a viable legacy modernization enabler
The three-phase workflow—Research (AI analysis), Review (human validation), Rebuild (guided generation)—ensures quality while dramatically accelerating delivery
Human expertise remains essential: domain experts must validate AI analysis, define clear intent, and review generated code to catch subtle issues like unnecessary optimizations
Interrupting AI mid-generation degrades output quality; clear upfront requirements and letting AI complete its task yields better results than constant course corrections
Well-structured prompts with coding standards, style guides, and clear test criteria minimize ambiguity and produce more consistent, maintainable code
money_quotes: Until recently, I held the belief that Generative Artificial Intelligence (GenAI) in software development was predominantly suited for greenfield projects. However, the introduction of the Model Context Protocol (MCP) marks a significant shift in this paradigm.
Modernization is not merely about replacing outdated technologies—it is about reimagining the future of the system and the business it supports.

The allure of LLMs is undeniable; they offer a seemingly effortless solution to complex problems... This should not create a bias in the experts, succumbing to the allure of LLMs and eventually take their hands off the wheel.
LLMs, at their core, are predictive sequence generators that build narratives token by token. When you interrupt a model mid-stream to course-correct, you break the logical flow it was constructing.
The goal is not to replace human expertise but to amplify it—to accelerate delivery timelines while ensuring that quality and maintainability are preserved, if not improved, during the transition.

This capability opens up the possibility for any individuals with decent technical expertise and strong domain knowledge to migrate any legacy codebase to a modern stack with minimal effort and in significantly less time.
key_insight: AI-assisted legacy migration succeeds not through brute-force code generation, but through structured workflows where human expertise validates AI analysis and guides generation with clear intent.
